title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Carla Morris, who performs in musical theatre.
audio_script: Interviewer: Carla Morris is here today. She performs in musical theatre. Carla, how did you get started? || Guest: My parents both sing, so people imagine they pushed me onto the stage, but they didn't. I was shy at school and preferred helping backstage. One day another student was ill before a small show. [20]The teacher asked me to sing her part, and the feeling of telling a story through music surprised me[/20]. After that I joined the drama club. || Interviewer: What was difficult when you trained professionally? || Guest: I thought dancing would be the hardest because I hadn't done much before. It was tiring, but I improved. [21]The real challenge was acting naturally while singing, instead of just trying to make a nice sound[/21]. That took practice. || Interviewer: How do you prepare for a new role? || Guest: First I read the script without music. Then I listen to the songs. Some performers copy recordings, but I avoid that. [22]I try to understand why the character says each line before deciding how to sing it[/22]. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy about touring? || Guest: Seeing new places is fun, though hotels can all look the same. The best thing is the audiences. [23]A joke that is quiet in one town can get a huge laugh somewhere else[/23], so every performance feels fresh. || Interviewer: Is there anything you dislike about your job? || Guest: People expect me to say travelling, but I can manage that. [24]What I find hardest is keeping my voice healthy when everyone around me has a cold[/24]. I have to be very careful. || Interviewer: What would you like to do in the future? || Guest: I'd love to keep performing, but [25]one day I want to direct a show for teenagers who don't think theatre is for them[/25]. I used to feel like that, so it matters to me. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Carla, people sometimes think musical theatre is only about big voices. Is that fair? || Guest: Not really. A strong voice helps, of course, but the audience must believe the person on stage. If you sing beautifully but nobody understands what your character wants, the song doesn't work. I spend a lot of time thinking about small movements and where to look during a song. Those details can be as important as the high notes. || Interviewer: How do you relax after a performance? || Guest: It takes me a while to calm down. Some actors can go straight home and sleep, but I can't. I usually make tea, remove my stage make-up slowly and write a few notes about the show. If something went wrong, I write it down and then stop worrying about it. Otherwise I would think about it all night. || Interviewer: Do you enjoy watching musicals when you are not performing? || Guest: Yes, but I watch them differently now. I notice lighting changes, costume problems and how performers save their energy. I still enjoy the story, though. When a show is good, I forget to analyse it after ten minutes, and that is the best feeling. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Carla Morris is here today. She performs in musical theatre. Carla, how did you get started?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My parents both sing, so people imagine they pushed me onto the stage, but they didn't. I was shy at school and preferred helping backstage. One day another student was ill before a small show. The teacher asked me to sing her part, and the feeling of telling a story through music surprised me. After that I joined the drama club.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was difficult when you trained professionally?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I thought dancing would be the hardest because I hadn't done much before. It was tiring, but I improved. The real challenge was acting naturally while singing, instead of just trying to make a nice sound. That took practice.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you prepare for a new role?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: First I read the script without music. Then I listen to the songs. Some performers copy recordings, but I avoid that. I try to understand why the character says each line before deciding how to sing it.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy about touring?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Seeing new places is fun, though hotels can all look the same. The best thing is the audiences. A joke that is quiet in one town can get a huge laugh somewhere else, so every performance feels fresh.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Is there anything you dislike about your job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People expect me to say travelling, but I can manage that. What I find hardest is keeping my voice healthy when everyone around me has a cold. I have to be very careful.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you like to do in the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'd love to keep performing, but one day I want to direct a show for teenagers who don't think theatre is for them. I used to feel like that, so it matters to me.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Carla, people sometimes think musical theatre is only about big voices. Is that fair?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not really. A strong voice helps, of course, but the audience must believe the person on stage. If you sing beautifully but nobody understands what your character wants, the song doesn't work. I spend a lot of time thinking about small movements and where to look during a song. Those details can be as important as the high notes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you relax after a performance?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It takes me a while to calm down. Some actors can go straight home and sleep, but I can't. I usually make tea, remove my stage make-up slowly and write a few notes about the show. If something went wrong, I write it down and then stop worrying about it. Otherwise I would think about it all night.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you enjoy watching musicals when you are not performing?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, but I watch them differently now. I notice lighting changes, costume problems and how performers save their energy. I still enjoy the story, though. When a show is good, I forget to analyse it after ten minutes, and that is the best feeling.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: How did Carla first become interested in performing?

options.A: Her parents encouraged her to sing.
options.C: She replaced someone in a school show.

questions.explanation: Carla says she sang another student's part when that student was ill, and the experience surprised her.

questions.question: What did Carla find most difficult during training?

options.A: singing and acting at the same time

questions.explanation: She says the real challenge was acting naturally while singing.

questions.question: What does Carla do first when preparing a song?
questions.explanation: Carla says she tries to understand why the character says each line before deciding how to sing it.

questions.question: What does Carla like about touring?

options.B: visiting new cities in the day

questions.explanation: She says audiences react differently, so every performance feels fresh.

questions.question: What does Carla find hardest about her job?

options.C: working with people who are ill

questions.explanation: Carla says she finds it hardest to keep her voice healthy when people around her have colds.

questions.question: What would Carla like to do in the future?

options.C: write a musical about her life

questions.explanation: She says she wants to direct a show for teenagers who do not think theatre is for them.
